title: Chapter 1: The Corpus
text:
Corpus selection and preperation ook nog over samen doen en region toevoegen
The corpus for this analysis is a combination of two playlists from spotify: “West Side Classics” and “East Coast Classics”. These playlists were chosen, because they were very popular on spotify, with 141.754 and 40.882 likes respectivally. As there is no golden standard for what songs are considered “West Coast” or “East Coast”, this method of popularity was used, which is similair to methods used by Boros (2025) and Gilbers et al. (2019).
In the West Coast (W.C.) playlist, some popular East Coast (E.C.) artist appeard. To mitigate this problem, artist that were in both datasets were noted and placed in their corresponding Coast. The artist ‘Outkast’ also appeared in both datasets and was removed as Outkast originated from Atlanta (not W.C. nor E.C.). Moreover, “West Side Classics” contained 84 songs, while “East Coast Classics” contained 177 after the previous step of moving around songs. As this is a difference of more that half of the songs, the E.C. set was downsampled to the W.C. set, both now containing 84 songs.


Track selection
To make analysis about track level information, two tracks per coast were selected. These songs needed to be representative for the range of music of every coast. To select these without randomly choosing two, one is the most respresentative for that coast and the other is a track distinctive for its coast, far from the other coast and not an outlier in its own coast. The latter was chosen so that the most distinctive track from a coast is not a general outlier (i.e. the most distinctive song for a coast could be a song that does not represent either coasts). First, all numerical features except “Popularity” (as this measure is changing) from the Spotify API were normalised. Second, the centroid (i.e. the mean over all features) of the data was calculated. To select the most representative song, the euclidean distance from each song to the centroid was calculated, and the song with the smallest distance was selected. To select the most distinctive song, the euclidean distance from each song to the other coast’s centroid was calculated. From that, the distance to its own coast’s centroid was subtracted. The song that was the furthest from the other coast’s centroid, while being close to its own coast’s centroid was selected. The songs that were selected are:

title: Week 10
text:
Homework week 10
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, American hip-hop was marked by a West Coast vs. East Coast feud, with fans strongly aligned to one side. The rivalry ultimately led to the deaths of Tupac and Biggie. For this week, I made a chordagram for the song ‘Give me the Loot’ by The Notorius B.I.G. I chose this song as it is statistically the most east-coast, meaning its audio profile sits closest to the overall average of all East Coast tracks (Only considering spotify’s features). ::: {.card} #### Listen to the song    :::









This is a chordagram of Give me the Loot by The Notorius B.I.G. In the chrodagram, you can see that the overall color is greenish, and that there are a lot of soft horizontal blue lines. The fact that the overall color is greenish and, for example, not bright yellow in most parts, tells us that the sound of this song is very rich. When listening to the song, there is a soft background sample overlayed with harder drums and ofcourse Biggies prominent voice. Together with that, there are a lot of sounds going in and out of the song: alarm like souds, record scratches and other voices. In the graph, you can also see a lot of vertical lines. Oftentimes in the song, you hear the samples and drums stop, only keeping Biggies voice. These stops correspond to the vertical lines, as there is a sudden stop of a lot of chords.

title: Week 9
text:
Homework week 9

Introduction
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, American hip-hop was marked by a West Coast vs. East Coast feud, with fans strongly aligned to one side. The rivalry ultimately led to the deaths of Tupac and Biggie. For this week, I made visualisations for the song ‘Bitch please’ by Snoop Dogg and Xzibit. I chose this song as it is statistically the most west-coast, meaning its audio profile sits closest to the overall average of all West Coast tracks (Only considering spotifies features).


Chromagram



Bitch Please chromagram


In this chromagram, you can see that the song is mostly in the keys B and F#, with G being high in the beginning of the song, but becoming less prominant later. This repetitiveness of pitches (i.e. the B and F#) can be explained by the fact that most rap songs have a looped sample, which repeat throughout the whole song


SelfSM timbre features



Bitch Please SSM


In this self-similarity matrix, you can see that the song features a highly dense, checkerboard-like pattern across the entire track, with consistent horizontal and vertical stripes indicating regular, repeating intervals of high similarity. This intense repetitiveness in the song’s timbre can be explained by the fact that most rap songs, particularly from this era of West Coast hip-hop, are built on a continuous, looped instrumental beat, meaning the underlying drum textures, basslines, and instrumentation repeat almost entirely unchanged throughout the whole song.
